name: Triple Double (Biles II)
technicalName: Triple-Double / Double Salto with Triple Twist
description: The Biles II is the hardest skill in women's gymnastics — in the world. Named after Simone Biles, it involves two backflips with three twists simultaneously. No one else in competitive gymnastics has done this skill. When Simone performs it, it's one of those moments where the entire arena falls silent and then erupts in disbelief. This skill has its own letter value that didn't exist in the Code of Points before Biles.
whatToLookFor: This skill is so rare you will only see Simone Biles do it — any video of it is worth showing
Two complete backflips AND three complete twists happening at the same time
An almost incomprehensible amount of rotation packed into a single skill
The landing happening on the spring floor after all that spinning
Pure power, speed, and precision throughout
commonMistakes: This skill has no common mistakes in the normal sense — very few people can attempt it
If your daughter is learning this, her coach has things well in hand

quickTipsChecklist.description: Maximum power, maximum height, maximum body tension

videos.title: Biles — Triple Double at World Championships

name: Split Leap
description: A split leap is when your daughter takes off from one foot and reaches a full split (180-degree leg split) in the air before landing. It's a dance element required in every level of gymnastics. A beautiful split leap looks like your daughter is flying — back leg parallel to the floor, front leg reaching forward, body upright and elegant.
whatToLookFor: A 180-degree split — both legs fully extended and level with each other
Good height — she should visibly leave the ground
An upright torso — not leaning forward over the front leg
Pointed toes on both feet throughout the leap
A graceful, confident landing on the front leg
commonMistakes: A 'bent' split where the back leg hasn't kicked up high enough — judges measure the angle
Leaning forward with the chest over the front leg
Flexed (bent-back) feet instead of pointed toes — this is a clear deduction
Not getting enough height, making the split hard to achieve in the air
Landing heavily with no grace or control

quickTipsChecklist.description: Both legs fully extended and level in the air
Chest doesn't lean forward over the front leg
Both feet have beautiful pointed toes
Visibly leaves the ground with the leap

videos.title: Split Leap Drill — How to Improve Your Split Leap

name: Switch Leap
description: A switch leap starts like a split leap but adds a leg switch in the air — your daughter takes off with the right leg forward, then switches so the left leg is forward when she lands. It requires more air time and coordination than a basic split leap and demonstrates that your daughter has good spatial awareness and flexibility.
whatToLookFor: A clear switch of the legs in the air — the leading leg changes
A full split position achieved after the switch (not just after takeoff)
Good height to have time for the switch to occur
Pointed toes on both feet
Landing on the correct foot (the one that came forward after the switch)
commonMistakes: The switch not happening — it just looks like a regular split leap
The split only happening before the switch, not after (wrong timing)
Not enough height for the switch to occur cleanly
One or both feet flexed (not pointed) during the leap
Landing off-balance after the switch

quickTipsChecklist.description: Legs clearly change front-to-back in the air
Full split achieved after the leg change
Enough height for the switch to happen cleanly
Feet stay pointed throughout

name: Fouetté Turn
description: Named from ballet, a fouetté turn involves whipping the leg around to generate spin — your daughter kicks her working leg out to the side and then brings it in repeatedly to create or maintain rotation. Multiple fouetté turns in a row (like a chain of turns) are called 'fouetté series' and show advanced dance skill.
whatToLookFor: The whipping leg motion that drives each turn
Rising up on the supporting leg's toes for the spin
A consistent spot (focus point) — she should snap her head around with each turn
Maintaining the same height and position with each turn in a series
A controlled finish that ends cleanly
commonMistakes: Not spotting — the head should snap around sharply to a single focus point each turn
Sinking down on the supporting leg instead of staying on high toes
The whipping leg going too high or not high enough
Traveling across the floor instead of turning in place
The series getting worse as it goes — the last turn should be as good as the first

quickTipsChecklist.description: Head snaps to focus point with each rotation
Stays high on toes throughout the series
Same leg motion drives every turn

name: Straddle Jump
description: A straddle jump is when your daughter jumps straight up and spreads her legs out to the sides in the air, reaching for her toes. It's a staple of any floor routine and is one of the first jumps gymnasts learn. The wider the spread and the more she can reach toward her feet, the better the score.
whatToLookFor: A high jump — she should clearly leave the ground
Legs spreading wide out to the sides (the wider the better)
Arms reaching toward the toes
Pointed toes on both feet
Two-foot landing with control (no stumbling or extra steps)
commonMistakes: A small jump that doesn't give enough air time for the straddle
Legs not going wide enough — a narrow straddle looks weak
Feet flexed (toes not pointed) — an automatic deduction
Not reaching toward the feet — arms hanging down at the sides
Landing on one foot or stumbling forward

quickTipsChecklist.description: Jumps as high as possible
Legs spread far out to the sides
Arms stretch toward feet
Both feet pointed throughout the jump

name: Double Layout (Floor)
description: Two backflips in a row with a completely straight body position — the hardest commonly competed tumbling pass in elite women's gymnastics (outside the Biles family of skills). The straight body position rotates more slowly than tucked or piked, so your daughter must generate incredible power and height. Simone Biles and very few others compete this skill regularly.
whatToLookFor: A spectacularly powerful approach — the fastest, most explosive run of her tumbling
A completely straight body for BOTH flips — no bend anywhere
Remarkable height — you should see her well above the floor at the peak
Two distinct flips clearly visible
A landing that shows she's in control after all that rotation
commonMistakes: Not generating enough power — attempting this without adequate strength and training is dangerous
Piking during the second flip — the layout must be maintained for both
Insufficient height, causing the second flip to be completed very close to the ground
Over-rotating and crashing forward
Only completing 1.5 layouts

quickTipsChecklist.description: Absolute maximum speed and power into the tumbling
Perfect layout maintained for both rotations
Rises far above the floor
Both rotations clearly distinguishable

name: Wolf Turn (Floor)
description: The floor wolf turn is the same skill as on beam but on the spring floor — your daughter squats on one leg with the other extended out to the side and spins. The spring floor makes balancing easier than beam but the turn still requires flexibility, balance, and the ability to spot quickly. Multiple wolf turns in a row show high difficulty.
whatToLookFor: A clear squat position — the bent leg should be at 90 degrees
The extended leg held straight throughout
A smooth, consistent spin with good speed
The number of rotations matching what was attempted (1, 2, or more turns)
A clean finish without stumbling or stepping
commonMistakes: Rising up too high — should be in a low wolf position, not standing
The extended leg bending at the knee
Stopping before completing all rotations
Traveling across the floor while turning (should spin in place)
Losing balance at the end

quickTipsChecklist.description: Squatting leg at 90°, low position
Side leg stays fully extended
Every rotation visibly completes
